title: Longest Common Subsequence (Strings)
description: Given s and t, return LCS length.

examples.explanation: Start: lcs(0,0). s[0]='A', t[0]='G' mismatch → max(lcs(1,0), lcs(0,1)). Branch 1: lcs(1,0): s[1]='G', t[0]='G' match → 1+lcs(2,1). lcs(2,1): s[2]='G', t[1]='X' mismatch → max(lcs(3,1), lcs(2,2)). This continues exploring. Branch 2: lcs(0,1): s[0]='A', t[1]='X' mismatch → max(lcs(1,1), lcs(0,2)). The recursion explores all paths. Final maximum found: sequence "GTAB" length 4.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all subsequences of both strings by comparing characters at each index, branching on matches or mismatches. It is simple to implement but impractical for large inputs due to exponential time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Longest Common Subsequence
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all subsequence combinations of two strings.
ladders.explanation: The brute force recursion approach solves LCS by systematically exploring every possible subsequence combination between two strings s and t. The intuition is that at each position (i,j), we have three possibilities: 1) If characters match, we can include them in the subsequence and move forward in both strings. 2) If they don't match, we must explore two branches: skip character from s, or skip character from t. This creates a binary decision tree where each level represents a character comparison. The algorithm works because it exhaustively checks every subsequence pairing, guaranteeing we'll find the longest common one. The base cases occur when we reach the end of either string (empty subsequence). The recursive relation is: LCS(i,j) = 1 + LCS(i+1,j+1) if s[i]==t[j], else max(LCS(i+1,j), LCS(i,j+1)). Time complexity is O(2^(n+m)) because each character comparison can branch into two recursive calls in the worst case. Space complexity is O(n+m) for the recursion stack. This connects to the parent problem by providing the complete solution framework. Common mistakes include: forgetting base cases, incorrectly handling indices, and not understanding the branching logic. Real code pattern: def lcs(i,j): if i>=len(s) or j>=len(t): return 0; if s[i]==t[j]: return 1+lcs(i+1,j+1); else: return max(lcs(i+1,j), lcs(i,j+1)).

examples.trace: Start: lcs(0,0). s[0]='A', t[0]='G' mismatch → max(lcs(1,0), lcs(0,1)). Branch 1: lcs(1,0): s[1]='G', t[0]='G' match → 1+lcs(2,1). lcs(2,1): s[2]='G', t[1]='X' mismatch → max(lcs(3,1), lcs(2,2)). This continues exploring. Branch 2: lcs(0,1): s[0]='A', t[1]='X' mismatch → max(lcs(1,1), lcs(0,2)). The recursion explores all paths. Final maximum found: sequence "GTAB" length 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Character Comparison Function
ladders.desc: The recursive function that compares characters at indices i,j and branches accordingly.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core recursive logic for comparing characters at positions i in string s and j in string t. The intuition is that LCS can be built incrementally by making decisions at each character pair. The function takes two indices and returns the LCS length from that position onward. The algorithm: Base case: if i or j is beyond string length, return 0 (no more characters). Recursive case: If s[i]==t[j], include this match (add 1) and recurse with i+1,j+1. If mismatch, we must explore both possibilities: exclude s[i] (recurse i+1,j) or exclude t[j] (recurse i,j+1), taking the maximum. This branching captures all subsequence combinations. Time complexity for this function alone is O(2^(n+m)) in worst case due to exponential branching. Space is O(n+m) for call stack. This feeds into L0 as the main recursive engine. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices, incorrect base case conditions, not returning max of both branches. Real pattern: def compare(i,j): if i>=len(s) or j>=len(t): return 0; if s[i]==t[j]: return 1+compare(i+1,j+1); else: return max(compare(i+1,j), compare(i,j+1)).

examples.trace: compare(0,0): s[0]='A', t[0]='A' match → 1+compare(1,1). compare(1,1): s[1]='B', t[1]='C' mismatch → max(compare(2,1), compare(1,2)). compare(2,1): i=2 (end) → 0. compare(1,2): j=2 (end) → 0. max(0,0)=0. So compare(1,1)=0. Thus compare(0,0)=1+0=1.

compare(0,0): s[0]='X', t[0]='Y' mismatch → max(compare(1,0), compare(0,1)). Branch1: compare(1,0): s[1]='Y', t[0]='Y' match → 1+compare(2,1). compare(2,1): i=2 → 0. So compare(1,0)=1. Branch2: compare(0,1): s[0]='X', t[1]='X' match → 1+compare(1,2). compare(1,2): j=2 → 0. So compare(0,1)=1. max(1,1)=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Branching on Character Match/Mismatch
ladders.desc: The decision logic: when characters match, include them; when they don't, explore both skip options.
ladders.explanation: This core logic defines how to handle each character comparison during recursion. The intuition is that building LCS involves making optimal choices at each step: if characters match, they should be included in the common subsequence because they contribute directly to length. If they don't match, we cannot include both, so we must try skipping one from either string to find other matches. The algorithm: At position (i,j), check s[i] and t[j]. If equal, return 1 + recurse(i+1,j+1). If not equal, return max(recurse(i+1,j), recurse(i,j+1)). This branching ensures we explore all possibilities. Time complexity for this logic unit is O(1) per call, but it drives exponential overall time. Space is O(1) per call. This feeds into L1 as the central comparison mechanism. Common mistakes: Not taking max when mismatched, incorrectly adding 1 on mismatch, misunderstanding the skip logic. Real pattern: if s[i]==t[j]: return 1+... else: return max(...).

examples.trace: Characters match: 'X' == 'X'. Decision: Include in subsequence. Action: Add 1 to count, move both indices forward (i+1, j+1).

Characters mismatch: 'M' != 'N'. Decision: Cannot include both. Explore two options: 1) Skip s[i]: recurse with i+1,j (keep t[j]). 2) Skip t[j]: recurse with i,j+1 (keep s[i]). Take maximum result from both branches.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function with Base Cases
ladders.desc: Implementing a recursive function that handles termination conditions.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a recursive function that knows when to stop. The intuition is that recursion must have base cases to prevent infinite loops; for LCS, we stop when we've exhausted one or both strings. The algorithm: Define function f(i,j). Base cases: If i reaches len(s) or j reaches len(t), return 0 (no characters left to compare). Recursive step: Otherwise, proceed with character comparison. This ensures the function terminates. Time complexity depends on recursive calls but base case check is O(1). Space is O(1) per call plus stack. This feeds into L2 by providing the recursive framework. Common mistakes: Missing base cases, incorrect condition (using == instead of >=), not handling empty strings. Real pattern: def f(i,j): if i>=len(s) or j>=len(t): return 0; # rest of logic.

examples.trace: f(0,0): Check base: j=0, len(t)=0 → j>=len(t) is true (0>=0). So return 0 immediately. No recursion.
f(0,1): Check base: i=0, len(s)=0 → i>=len(s) true. Return 0. Even though j=1, we stop because s is empty.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Characters
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of checking if two characters are equal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is simply comparing two characters from strings s and t. The intuition is that LCS construction starts with individual character comparisons; if they match, they can be part of the common subsequence. The algorithm: Access character at index i in s and index j in t using s[i] and t[j]. Compare them using equality operator (==). Return True if equal, False otherwise. This is an O(1) time and O(1) space operation. This feeds into L3 as the first step in the recursive function. Common mistakes: Comparing indices instead of characters, using assignment (=) instead of equality (==), out-of-bounds access. Real pattern: if s[i] == t[j]: # do something.

examples.trace: s[0] = 'c', t[0] = 'd'. Compare: 'c' == 'd'? False.
s[1] = 'p', t[1] = 'p'. Compare: 'p' == 'p'? True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic knowledge of strings as sequences of characters accessible by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the prerequisite concept of strings and indexing. A string is a sequence of characters (e.g., "hello"). Each character has a position called an index, starting from 0. For example, in s="cat", s[0]='c', s[1]='a', s[2]='t'. The length of a string is the number of characters; for "cat", len(s)=3. Indices must be within range 0 to len(s)-1 to avoid errors. This is essential for LCS because we need to access and compare characters at specific positions. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a basic concept. This feeds into L4 by enabling character access. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing, accessing out-of-bounds indices, confusing string length with last index. Real pattern: s = "example"; char = s[i] where i is integer index.

examples.trace: String "dog" has indices: 0:'d', 1:'o', 2:'g'. Access s[1] returns character at position 1, which is 'o'.
Empty string has length 0, so valid indices are none. Accessing s[0] tries to get character at index 0, but since string is empty, this causes an index error.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store results of subproblems (i, j) in a hash table, avoiding repeated work. This top-down approach improves over brute force by caching intermediate results.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Common Subsequence with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to find LCS length.
ladders.explanation: The Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) problem asks for the length of the longest subsequence common to two strings s and t. A subsequence maintains order but not necessarily contiguity. The intuition is that we can break this into overlapping subproblems: comparing prefixes of s and t. If characters match, we can extend the LCS; if not, we try skipping a character from either string. Memoization stores results of subproblems (i,j) where i and j are indices into s and t, avoiding exponential recomputation. The algorithm uses recursion: base cases are when either string is empty (return 0). For indices i and j, if s[i]==t[j], recursively compute for i+1,j+1 and add 1. Otherwise, take the max of skipping s[i] (i+1,j) or t[j] (i,j+1). Store results in a 2D memo dictionary. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n and m are string lengths, as each subproblem is computed once. Space is O(n*m) for memo storage plus recursion stack O(n+m). This top-down approach is intuitive but requires careful handling of indices and memo lookup. Common mistakes include not using memo correctly (e.g., forgetting to return stored values), off-by-one errors in indices, and misunderstanding subsequence vs. substring. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete recursive function with memoization. Real code pattern: define a helper function lcs(i,j) that checks memo, handles base cases, computes result recursively, stores it, and returns it. Initialize memo as a dictionary and call lcs(0,0).

examples.trace: Call lcs(0,0): s[0]='a', t[0]='a' match, so result = 1 + lcs(1,1). lcs(1,1): s[1]='b', t[1]='c' no match, so result = max(lcs(2,1), lcs(1,2)). Compute lcs(2,1): s[2]='c', t[1]='c' match, so 1 + lcs(3,2). lcs(3,2): s[3]='d', t[2]='e' no match, so max(lcs(4,2), lcs(3,3)). lcs(4,2): s[4]='e', t[2]='e' match, so 1 + lcs(5,3). lcs(5,3): base case (i=5 end of s), return 0. So lcs(4,2)=1. lcs(3,3): s[3]='d', t[3] out of bounds (len(t)=3), base case return 0. So lcs(3,2)=max(1,0)=1. Thus lcs(2,1)=1+1=2. Now lcs(1,2): s[1]='b', t[2]='e' no match, so max(lcs(2,2), lcs(1,3)). lcs(2,2): s[2]='c', t[2]='e' no match, so max(lcs(3,2), lcs(2,3)). lcs(3,2)=1 as computed. lcs(2,3): base case (j=3 end of t), return 0. So lcs(2,2)=max(1,0)=1. lcs(1,3): base case (j=3), return 0. So lcs(1,2)=max(1,0)=1. Thus lcs(1,1)=max(2,1)=2. So lcs(0,0)=1+2=3. Memo stores values for all (i,j) pairs encountered.

Call lcs(0,0): s[0]='a', t[0]='d' no match, so result = max(lcs(1,0), lcs(0,1)). Compute lcs(1,0): s[1]='b', t[0]='d' no match, so max(lcs(2,0), lcs(1,1)). lcs(2,0): s[2]='c', t[0]='d' no match, so max(lcs(3,0), lcs(2,1)). lcs(3,0): base case (i=3), return 0. lcs(2,1): s[2]='c', t[1]='e' no match, so max(lcs(3,1), lcs(2,2)). lcs(3,1): base case, return 0. lcs(2,2): s[2]='c', t[2]='f' no match, so max(lcs(3,2), lcs(2,3)). Both base cases return 0. So lcs(2,2)=0. Thus lcs(2,1)=max(0,0)=0. So lcs(2,0)=max(0,0)=0. Now lcs(1,1): s[1]='b', t[1]='e' no match, so max(lcs(2,1), lcs(1,2)). lcs(2,1)=0 as above. lcs(1,2): s[1]='b', t[2]='f' no match, so max(lcs(2,2), lcs(1,3)). lcs(2,2)=0, lcs(1,3) base case 0. So lcs(1,2)=0. Thus lcs(1,1)=max(0,0)=0. So lcs(1,0)=max(0,0)=0. Similarly, lcs(0,1) will compute to 0. Thus lcs(0,0)=max(0,0)=0. All memo entries are 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive LCS Function with Memo Lookup
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes LCS for prefixes (i,j) with memoization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the approach: a function lcs(i,j) that returns the LCS length for suffixes s[i:] and t[j:]. The intuition is to solve smaller subproblems and combine results. The algorithm: if i or j is at the end of their string (i==len(s) or j==len(t)), return 0 (base case). First, check if (i,j) is already computed in memo (a dictionary); if yes, return the stored value. Otherwise, if s[i]==t[j], set result = 1 + lcs(i+1, j+1). If not equal, set result = max(lcs(i+1, j), lcs(i, j+1)). Store result in memo at key (i,j) and return it. Time complexity for this function call is O(1) per subproblem after memoization, but overall it drives O(n*m) total calls. Space is O(n*m) for memo. The connection to L0 is that this function is called recursively to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include incorrect base conditions (e.g., using i>len(s) instead of i==len(s)), not checking memo before computation (leading to repeated work), and wrong recursive calls (e.g., swapping i and j). Real code pattern: define lcs(i,j) with if (i,j) in memo: return memo[(i,j)]; if i==len(s) or j==len(t): return 0; if s[i]==t[j]: res=1+lcs(i+1,j+1); else: res=max(lcs(i+1,j), lcs(i,j+1)); memo[(i,j)]=res; return res.

examples.trace: Call lcs(0,0): memo empty. i=0,j=0, not at end. s[0]='a', t[0]='a' match, so res = 1 + lcs(1,1). Call lcs(1,1): i=1,j=1, not at end. s[1]='b', t[1]='c' no match, so res = max(lcs(2,1), lcs(1,2)). Call lcs(2,1): i=2 (end of s), base case return 0. Call lcs(1,2): i=1,j=2 (end of t), base case return 0. So lcs(1,1)=max(0,0)=0. Store memo[(1,1)]=0. Thus lcs(0,0)=1+0=1. Store memo[(0,0)]=1.

Call lcs(0,0): memo empty. s[0]='x', t[0]='x' match, so res=1+lcs(1,1). lcs(1,1): s[1]='y', t[1]='z' no match, so res=max(lcs(2,1), lcs(1,2)). lcs(2,1): base case (i=2) return 0. lcs(1,2): base case (j=2) return 0. So lcs(1,1)=0. Store memo[(1,1)]=0. Thus lcs(0,0)=1+0=1. Store memo[(0,0)]=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Relation for LCS
ladders.desc: Define the recurrence relation that breaks LCS into subproblems.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recurrence relation that defines LCS length L(i,j) for prefixes s[0:i] and t[0:j] (or suffixes s[i:] and t[j:]). Intuition: when comparing characters at positions i and j, if they match, we can include this character in the LCS and move to the next positions in both strings. If they don't match, we have two options: skip the character in s (i+1) or skip in t (j+1), and take the maximum. Formally, let L(i,j) be LCS length for s[i:] and t[j:]. Base case: if i==len(s) or j==len(t), L(i,j)=0. Recurrence: if s[i]==t[j], L(i,j)=1+L(i+1,j+1); else L(i,j)=max(L(i+1,j), L(i,j+1)). This relation exhibits optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems, making it suitable for dynamic programming. Time analysis: without memoization, this leads to exponential time O(2^(n+m)); with memoization, it becomes O(n*m). Space for storing results is O(n*m). The connection to L1 is that this relation is implemented in the recursive function. Common mistakes: misdefining base cases (e.g., using i<0), incorrect recurrence for non-matching case (e.g., using min instead of max), or misunderstanding prefix vs suffix indexing. Real code pattern: this is the logical rule used within lcs(i,j).

examples.trace: Compute L(0,0): s[0]='a', t[0]='a' match, so L(0,0)=1+L(1,1). Compute L(1,1): s[1]='b', t[1]='c' no match, so L(1,1)=max(L(2,1), L(1,2)). L(2,1): i=2 (end of s), base case 0. L(1,2): j=2 (end of t), base case 0. So L(1,1)=max(0,0)=0. Thus L(0,0)=1+0=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Memoization with Hash Table
ladders.desc: Implement a memoization cache to store and retrieve computed subproblem results.
ladders.explanation: Memoization is an optimization technique that stores results of expensive function calls and returns cached results when the same inputs occur again. For LCS, subproblems are identified by indices (i,j). The intuition is to avoid recomputing L(i,j) multiple times by saving it in a hash table (dictionary). The algorithm: initialize an empty dictionary memo. In the recursive function, before computing, check if (i,j) is a key in memo; if yes, return memo[(i,j)]. After computing the result, store it as memo[(i,j)] = result. This reduces time from exponential to polynomial. Time analysis: each (i,j) pair is computed once, so O(n*m) operations. Space is O(n*m) for the dictionary. The connection to L2 is that this cache stores values of the recurrence relation. Common mistakes: using a list instead of dictionary (less efficient for sparse accesses), not checking memo before computation, or storing incorrect keys (e.g., using strings instead of tuples). Real code pattern: memo = {}; in function, if (i,j) in memo: return memo[(i,j)]; at end, memo[(i,j)] = res; return res.

examples.input: Compute L(0,0) for s="ab", t="ac" with memo={}
examples.trace: Start memo empty. Call L(0,0): not in memo. Compute: match at i=0,j=0, so need L(1,1). Call L(1,1): not in memo. Compute: no match, so need L(2,1) and L(1,2). Call L(2,1): not in memo, base case 0, store memo[(2,1)]=0. Call L(1,2): not in memo, base case 0, store memo[(1,2)]=0. So L(1,1)=max(0,0)=0, store memo[(1,1)]=0. Thus L(0,0)=1+0=1, store memo[(0,0)]=1. Final memo has 4 entries.

examples.input: Compute L(0,0) for s="xy", t="xz" with memo={}
examples.trace: Similar to above: L(0,0) calls L(1,1) which calls L(2,1) and L(1,2). All base cases return 0 and are stored. L(1,1)=0 stored, L(0,0)=1 stored.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Indexing and Comparison
ladders.desc: Access characters in strings and compare them for equality.
ladders.explanation: This foundational operation involves retrieving characters from strings using indices and checking if they are equal. In Python, strings are immutable sequences, and you can access character at index i with s[i]. Indices start at 0. Comparison uses the == operator. For LCS, we need to compare s[i] and t[j] to decide the recursive step. The intuition is simple: if characters match, they can be part of the common subsequence. Time complexity: O(1) per access and comparison. Space: O(1). The connection to L3 is that this operation is used within the recurrence to determine matching. Common mistakes: index out of range (e.g., accessing beyond string length), confusing 0-based indexing, or comparing case-sensitively when not intended. Real code pattern: if i < len(s) and j < len(t): if s[i] == t[j]: ... else: ...

examples.trace: String s has indices: 0:'h',1:'e',2:'l',3:'l',4:'o'. So s[1] is 'e'. String t: 0:'w',1:'o',2:'r',3:'l',4:'d'. So t[2] is 'r'. Compare 'e' == 'r'? False.
s[0] is first character 'a'. t[0] is first character 'a'. Compare 'a' == 'a'? True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Recursion Base Case
ladders.desc: Understand the simplest condition that stops recursion: when a string is empty.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a method where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem. A base case is the condition under which recursion stops, preventing infinite loops. For LCS, when we reach the end of either string (i.e., no characters left to compare), the LCS length is 0 because an empty string has no subsequence. Intuition: if one string is empty, there can be no common characters. The base case is defined as: if i == len(s) or j == len(t), return 0. Here, len(s) gives the length of string s. Time and space are O(1) for this check. The connection to L4 is that this uses string length and indices. Common mistakes: forgetting base case (leads to infinite recursion), using incorrect condition (e.g., i > len(s)), or not handling both strings. Real code pattern: if i == len(s) or j == len(t): return 0.

examples.trace: s is empty string, so len(s)=0. i=0, which equals len(s)=0, so base case triggered: return 0. No need to check t.
t is empty, len(t)=0. j=0, which equals len(t)=0, so base case triggered: return 0.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Dynamic Programming Bottom-Up O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Iteratively fill a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] represents LCS length for prefixes s[0:i] and t[0:j], using recurrence based on character matches. This is the standard efficient solution with clear tabulation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Common Subsequence using DP Table
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of LCS length using bottom-up dynamic programming with 2D table.
ladders.explanation: The Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) problem asks for the length of the longest sequence that appears in the same relative order in both strings, but not necessarily contiguously. The bottom-up DP approach solves this by building a 2D table where dp[i][j] represents the LCS length for prefixes s[0:i] and t[0:j]. The key insight is that we can compute dp[i][j] based on three possibilities: if characters match, we extend the LCS from previous prefixes; if they don't match, we take the maximum LCS from either skipping a character in s or t. We initialize dp[0][j] = 0 and dp[i][0] = 0 since empty strings have LCS length 0. The recurrence is: if s[i-1] == t[j-1], then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1; else dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). We fill the table row by row from i=1 to n and j=1 to m. The answer is dp[n][m]. Time complexity is O(n*m) for filling the table, and space complexity is O(n*m) for storing the table. This approach is efficient and guarantees optimal solution by exploring all possible subsequences through overlapping subproblems. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices (using s[i] instead of s[i-1]), incorrect initialization, or misunderstanding the recurrence. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete algorithm using the recurrence relation as the key subroutine.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence Relation for LCS
ladders.desc: Implement the recurrence relation that computes dp[i][j] based on character comparison.
ladders.explanation: The recurrence relation is the heart of the DP solution for LCS. It defines how to compute the LCS length for prefixes s[0:i] and t[0:j] using previously computed values. The intuition is that when comparing characters s[i-1] and t[j-1], if they match, we can extend the LCS from the prefixes without these characters (i.e., dp[i-1][j-1]) by 1. If they don't match, the LCS must come from either skipping s[i-1] (using dp[i-1][j]) or skipping t[j-1] (using dp[i][j-1]), and we take the maximum of these two options. This works because we're considering all possibilities: including the matching character or excluding one of the non-matching characters. The algorithm is: for given i and j (1-indexed for dp table), if s[i-1] == t[j-1], set dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1; else set dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Time complexity for computing one cell is O(1), and space is O(1) per cell, but overall it's used in a loop. This sub-routine feeds into L0 by being applied repeatedly to fill the entire DP table. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (mixing 0-based and 1-based), or not handling the base cases (i=0 or j=0) which are handled separately in initialization. The real code pattern is a conditional check within nested loops.

examples.trace: We compute dp[1][1] for prefixes s[0:1]='a' and t[0:1]='a'. Compare s[0]='a' and t[0]='a': they match. So dp[1][1] = dp[0][0] + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1.
Compute dp[2][1] for prefixes s[0:2]='ab' and t[0:1]='c'. Compare s[1]='b' and t[0]='c': no match. So dp[2][1] = max(dp[1][1]=0, dp[2][0]=0) = max(0,0) = 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Character Matching and Subsequence Extension
ladders.desc: Understand how matching characters extend the LCS and how to handle mismatches.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of LCS revolves around comparing characters from two strings and deciding how to build the common subsequence. When characters match, it indicates that this character can be included in the LCS, so we extend the LCS from the previous prefixes (excluding these characters) by 1. This works because if s[i-1] == t[j-1], then the best LCS for prefixes up to i and j must include this matching character at the end, so we add it to the LCS from prefixes i-1 and j-1. When characters don't match, we cannot include both in the LCS simultaneously, so we consider two options: skip the character from s (look at dp[i-1][j]) or skip the character from t (look at dp[i][j-1]), and take the maximum. This ensures we don't miss any potential longer subsequence. The intuition is that we're exploring all possibilities: include if match, or exclude one if no match. This is a greedy-like decision that guarantees optimality due to the DP structure. The algorithm is a decision rule applied per character pair. Time and space are O(1) per comparison, but it's the fundamental operation repeated in the DP table. This feeds into L1 by being encapsulated in the recurrence relation. Common mistakes include trying to include both characters when they don't match, or not realizing that skipping a character is necessary. The real code pattern is an if-else block based on character equality.

examples.input: Match case: s_prefix="ab", t_prefix="ac", current chars s[1]='b', t[1]='c'?
examples.trace: Compare 'b' and 'c': they don't match. So we cannot include both. Options: skip 'b' (use LCS of "a" and "ac") or skip 'c' (use LCS of "ab" and "a"). We take the max of these two LCS lengths.

examples.input: Match case: s_prefix="xyz", t_prefix="xwz", current chars s[2]='z', t[2]='z'
examples.trace: Compare 'z' and 'z': they match. So we can include 'z' in the LCS. The LCS for prefixes "xy" and "xw" is 1 ("x"), so adding 'z' gives LCS length 2 ("xz").

ladders.title: Building Block — 2D Table Initialization and Iteration
ladders.desc: Set up and traverse a 2D DP table for prefix-based computations.
ladders.explanation: A 2D table is used in bottom-up DP to store solutions to subproblems. For LCS, we create a table dp with dimensions (n+1) x (m+1), where n and m are lengths of strings s and t. The extra row and column (index 0) represent empty prefixes. Initialization: dp[0][j] = 0 for all j from 0 to m, and dp[i][0] = 0 for all i from 0 to n, because the LCS with an empty string is always 0. This sets the base cases. Then, we iterate over i from 1 to n and j from 1 to m, filling each cell dp[i][j] using the recurrence relation. The iteration order is crucial: we must fill cells in such a way that when computing dp[i][j], the values dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], and dp[i][j-1] are already computed. A row-major order (outer loop over i, inner loop over j) works because it processes rows top to bottom and left to right, ensuring dependencies are met. The intuition is that we're building solutions from smaller prefixes to larger ones, leveraging previously computed results. Time complexity for initialization is O(n+m), and iteration is O(n*m). Space is O(n*m) for the table. This feeds into L2 by providing the structure to apply character matching logic across all prefixes. Common mistakes include incorrect table dimensions (forgetting the +1), wrong iteration bounds, or filling in wrong order. The real code pattern uses nested for-loops and a 2D list.

examples.trace: Create dp table with 3 rows (i=0 to 2) and 3 columns (j=0 to 2). Initialize: dp[0][0]=0, dp[0][1]=0, dp[0][2]=0, dp[1][0]=0, dp[2][0]=0. Then iterate: i=1,j=1: fill using recurrence; i=1,j=2: fill; i=2,j=1: fill; i=2,j=2: fill.
Create dp table with 2 rows (i=0 to 1) and 4 columns (j=0 to 3). Initialize: dp[0][0]=0, dp[0][1]=0, dp[0][2]=0, dp[0][3]=0, dp[1][0]=0. Then iterate: i=1,j=1: fill; i=1,j=2: fill; i=1,j=3: fill.

testCases.expected: dp size 3x3, first row and column 0
dp size 4x2, first row and column 0
dp size 2x5, first row and column 0
dp size 6x6, first row and column 0
dp size 3x4, first row and column 0
dp size 5x3, first row and column 0
dp size 11x11, first row and column 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Character Access and Comparison
ladders.desc: Access characters from strings and compare them for equality.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves retrieving individual characters from strings and checking if they are equal. In programming, strings are typically indexed starting from 0. For example, in string s = "hello", s[0] is 'h', s[1] is 'e', etc. To compare two characters, we use the equality operator (== in most languages). This is a fundamental step in many string algorithms, including LCS, where we need to compare s[i-1] and t[j-1] to decide if they match. The intuition is that characters are the basic units of strings, and comparing them allows us to identify matches that can contribute to common subsequences. The algorithm is straightforward: given indices i and j (within valid bounds), access s[i] and t[j] using array indexing, then compare with ==. Time complexity is O(1) per access and comparison, and space is O(1). This feeds into L3 by being used repeatedly during table iteration to apply the core logic. Common mistakes include accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., when i or j equal string length), or using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==). The real code pattern is a simple if statement with indexed string access.

examples.input: s = "cat", index i=1; t = "dog", index j=1
examples.trace: Access s at index 1: s[1] is the second character, 'a'. Access t at index 1: t[1] is 'o'. Compare 'a' == 'o': they are not equal, so result is false.

examples.input: s = "book", index i=0; t = "bag", index j=0
examples.trace: Access s at index 0: s[0] is first character, 'b'. Access t at index 0: t[0] is 'b'. Compare 'b' == 'b': they are equal, so result is true.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Subsequence?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition of a subsequence of a string.
ladders.explanation: A subsequence of a string is a sequence that can be derived from the original string by deleting some or no characters without changing the order of the remaining characters. It is different from a substring, which must be contiguous. For example, for the string "abc", some subsequences are "", "a", "b", "c", "ab", "ac", "bc", "abc". Note that "ac" is a subsequence but not a substring because it skips 'b'. The intuition is that a subsequence preserves the relative order but allows gaps. This concept is foundational for LCS because we are looking for the longest sequence common to two strings in terms of subsequences. The algorithm to generate all subsequences is exponential, but for LCS, we use DP to find the length efficiently. Understanding this definition helps in visualizing why we can skip characters in the DP approach. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a definition. This feeds into L4 by explaining why we compare individual characters in order. Common mistakes include confusing subsequence with substring, or thinking that characters must be consecutive. The real code pattern is conceptual, but one can think of it as selecting indices in increasing order.

examples.trace: Start with empty: "". Take first char: "a". Take second: "b". Take third: "c". Take first and second: "ab". Take first and third: "ac". Take second and third: "bc". Take all: "abc". Total 8 subsequences (including empty).
Check "hlo" against "hello": 'h' is at index 0, 'l' is at index 2 (skipping 'e'), 'o' is at index 4 (skipping another 'l'). All characters are in order, so yes, "hlo" is a subsequence of "hello".

approaches.name: Approach 4: Dynamic Programming with Space Optimization O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Optimize space by using only two rows or one row of the DP table, since each state depends only on previous rows. This reduces space usage while maintaining the same time complexity as standard DP.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Common Subsequence with Space Optimization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with space optimization to O(min(n,m))
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the Longest Common Subsequence problem using dynamic programming with space optimization. The standard DP approach uses an (n+1)×(m+1) table, but we can optimize space by noticing that each row only depends on the previous row. We maintain only two rows (current and previous) or even a single row with careful updating. The algorithm compares characters from both strings: if they match, we take the diagonal value plus 1; otherwise, we take the maximum of left and top values. By using only O(min(n,m)) space, we achieve the same O(n×m) time complexity as the standard DP approach. This optimization is crucial when dealing with large strings where memory constraints matter. The key insight is that we don't need the entire DP table—just enough information to compute the next row. We'll implement the two-row approach for clarity, though a single-row approach is also possible with careful variable management. The algorithm initializes two arrays representing DP rows, iterates through both strings, and updates values based on character matches. The final answer is in the last element of the current row. This approach maintains the optimal substructure property of LCS while dramatically reducing space usage.

examples.trace:
Initialize: prev=[0,0,0,0], curr=[0,0,0,0]

i=1 (s[0]='a'):
  j=1 (t[0]='a'): match! curr[1]=prev[0]+1=0+1=1
  j=2 (t[1]='c'): no match, curr[2]=max(curr[1]=1, prev[2]=0)=1
  j=3 (t[2]='e'): no match, curr[3]=max(curr[2]=1, prev[3]=0)=1
  Update: prev=curr.copy()=[0,1,1,1]

i=2 (s[1]='b'):
  Reset curr[0]=0
  j=1: no match, curr[1]=max(curr[0]=0, prev[1]=1)=1
  j=2: no match, curr[2]=max(curr[1]=1, prev[2]=1)=1
  j=3: no match, curr[3]=max(curr[2]=1, prev[3]=1)=1
  Update: prev=[0,1,1,1]

i=3 (s[2]='c'):
  Reset curr[0]=0
  j=1: no match, curr[1]=max(0,1)=1
  j=2: match! curr[2]=prev[1]+1=1+1=2
  j=3: no match, curr[3]=max(curr[2]=2, prev[3]=1)=2
  Update: prev=[0,1,2,2]

i=4 (s[3]='d'):
  Reset curr[0]=0
  j=1: no match, curr[1]=max(0,1)=1
  j=2: no match, curr[2]=max(curr[1]=1, prev[2]=2)=2
  j=3: no match, curr[3]=max(curr[2]=2, prev[3]=2)=2
  Update: prev=[0,1,2,2]

i=5 (s[4]='e'):
  Reset curr[0]=0
  j=1: no match, curr[1]=max(0,1)=1
  j=2: no match, curr[2]=max(curr[1]=1, prev[2]=2)=2
  j=3: match! curr[3]=prev[2]+1=2+1=3

Final answer: curr[3]=3

Initialize: prev=[0,0,0,0], curr=[0,0,0,0]

i=1 (s[0]='a'):
  j=1 (t[0]='d'): no match, curr[1]=max(curr[0]=0, prev[1]=0)=0
  j=2 (t[1]='e'): no match, curr[2]=max(curr[1]=0, prev[2]=0)=0
  j=3 (t[2]='f'): no match, curr[3]=max(curr[2]=0, prev[3]=0)=0
  Update: prev=[0,0,0,0]

i=2 (s[1]='b'):
  Reset curr[0]=0
  j=1: no match, curr[1]=max(0,0)=0
  j=2: no match, curr[2]=max(0,0)=0
  j=3: no match, curr[3]=max(0,0)=0
  Update: prev=[0,0,0,0]

i=3 (s[2]='c'):
  Reset curr[0]=0
  j=1: no match, curr[1]=max(0,0)=0
  j=2: no match, curr[2]=max(0,0)=0
  j=3: no match, curr[3]=max(0,0)=0

Final answer: curr[3]=0

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Two-Row DP Update for LCS
ladders.desc: Implement the core update logic that processes one character from s against all of t
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the heart of the space-optimized LCS algorithm: processing one character from string s against all characters of string t using only two rows. Given previous row (prev) representing DP values for processed characters of s, and current character s_char, we compute new row (curr) for s_char against all of t. The algorithm initializes curr[0]=0 (base case), then iterates through t characters. For each t_char: if s_char == t_char, curr[j] = prev[j-1] + 1 (diagonal value + 1 for match); otherwise, curr[j] = max(curr[j-1], prev[j]) (maximum of left and top values). After processing all t characters, prev is updated to curr for the next iteration. This sub-routine captures the recurrence relation: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 if s[i-1]==t[j-1], else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). The space optimization comes from using only prev and curr instead of full table. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(m) where m is length of t. Space is O(m) for the two rows. This feeds into the full problem by being called for each character of s. Common mistakes include forgetting to reset curr[0]=0 each iteration, incorrect indexing when accessing prev[j-1], or not properly updating prev after each iteration. The pseudocode clearly shows the iteration pattern and update logic.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Space Optimization Using Two Rows
ladders.desc: Understand how to reduce DP space from O(n×m) to O(min(n,m)) by tracking only necessary information
ladders.explanation: The core insight for space optimization in LCS DP is that each row in the DP table depends only on the previous row. In standard DP, we compute dp[i][j] using dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], and dp[i][j-1]. Notice that dp[i][j-1] is in the same row (i), while the other two are in row i-1. This means if we process row by row, we only need the current row and previous row at any time. We can maintain two arrays: prev for row i-1 and curr for row i. At each step, we compute curr[j] using prev[j-1], prev[j], and curr[j-1]. After finishing row i, we set prev = curr (copy values) and move to next row. This reduces space from O(n×m) to O(m) where m is length of t. We can further optimize to O(min(n,m)) by ensuring t is the shorter string. The intuition: LCS computation has a 'sliding window' property where only immediate previous results matter. This works because the recurrence has limited dependencies. Time remains O(n×m) since we still compute all n×m states. The connection to higher level: this optimization enables the full space-optimized solution. Common mistakes: using same array for prev and curr without proper copying (need deep copy or create new array), incorrect initialization of prev row (should be all zeros), or not handling empty strings. The pseudocode shows the two-array approach clearly.

examples.input: Standard DP table rows 0-2, show transition to two-row approach
examples.trace:
Full table for s="ab", t="ac":
Row 0: [0,0,0]
Row 1 (s[0]='a'): [0,1,1]
Row 2 (s[1]='b'): [0,1,1]

Two-row approach:
Initialize prev=[0,0,0]

Process s[0]='a':
  curr[0]=0
  j=1: 'a'=='a' → curr[1]=prev[0]+1=1
  j=2: 'a'!='c' → curr[2]=max(curr[1]=1, prev[2]=0)=1
  Update prev=curr.copy()=[0,1,1]

Process s[1]='b':
  curr[0]=0
  j=1: 'b'!='a' → curr[1]=max(curr[0]=0, prev[1]=1)=1
  j=2: 'b'!='c' → curr[2]=max(curr[1]=1, prev[2]=1)=1

Final: curr[2]=1 matches full table

examples.input: Show space reduction from 3×3 table to two rows
examples.trace:
Full 3×3 table (s length=2, t length=2): 3 rows × 3 cols = 9 elements

Two-row approach:
prev: array of length 3 (t length+1) = 3 elements
curr: array of length 3 = 3 elements
Total: 6 elements

Reduction: 9 → 6 (33% reduction)
For larger n,m: reduction from n×m to 2×m (or 2×n if we use shorter dimension)
Example: n=1000, m=1000
Full: 1,000,000 elements
Two-row: 2,000 elements (99.8% reduction)

testCases.input: n=3, m=3, full table size vs two-row size
n=5, m=2, full table size vs two-row size
n=1, m=10, full table size vs two-row size
n=100, m=100, full table size vs two-row size
n=0, m=5, full table size vs two-row size
prev=[0,1,1], compute curr for match at j=2
prev=[0,0,0], compute curr for no matches
prev=[0,2,3], curr[1]=1, compute curr[2] for non-match
Space for n=50, m=50 with optimization
Verify two-row works for s="", t="abc"

ladders.title: Building Block — DP Recurrence Relation for LCS
ladders.desc: Understand the mathematical recurrence that defines LCS length
ladders.explanation: The Longest Common Subsequence problem has optimal substructure, meaning the solution can be built from solutions to subproblems. Let dp[i][j] represent the length of LCS of prefixes s[0:i] and t[0:j] (where i,j are lengths, not indices). The recurrence relation is: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 if s[i-1] == t[j-1] (last characters match), otherwise dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Base cases: dp[0][j] = 0 for all j (empty s), dp[i][0] = 0 for all i (empty t). Intuition: if last characters match, they belong to LCS, so we add 1 to LCS of remaining prefixes. If they don't match, LCS is either LCS without s's last char or without t's last char—we take maximum. This recurrence works because LCS has the property that prefixes of LCS are LCS of prefixes. The relation is proven correct by induction. Time to compute all dp[i][j] is O(n×m). This building block feeds into space optimization because we need to implement this recurrence efficiently. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in indices (using s[i] instead of s[i-1]), incorrect base cases, or misunderstanding that dp[i][j] uses lengths not indices. The pseudocode shows the recurrence clearly in the inner loop.

examples.trace:
dp[2][2] means prefixes s[0:2]="ab", t[0:2]="ac"
Last chars: s[1]='b', t[1]='c' → no match
So dp[2][2] = max(dp[1][2], dp[2][1])

Compute dp[1][2]: prefixes s[0:1]="a", t[0:2]="ac"
Last chars: s[0]='a', t[1]='c' → no match
dp[1][2] = max(dp[0][2], dp[1][1])

Compute dp[0][2]: prefixes "", "ac" → 0
Compute dp[1][1]: prefixes "a", "a" → last chars match → dp[0][0]+1=0+1=1
So dp[1][2] = max(0,1)=1

Compute dp[2][1]: prefixes "ab", "a"
Last chars: s[1]='b', t[0]='a' → no match
dp[2][1] = max(dp[1][1], dp[2][0])
dp[1][1]=1 (computed), dp[2][0]=0
So dp[2][1]=max(1,0)=1

Finally dp[2][2]=max(1,1)=1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Characters and Taking Maximum
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations: character comparison and max() function
ladders.explanation: This level covers the two basic operations at the heart of LCS computation: comparing two characters for equality, and taking the maximum of two numbers. Character comparison is straightforward: check if s[i] == t[j]. In LCS context, we compare the current characters from both strings. If they match, we include this character in the subsequence. The max() operation determines which path yields longer subsequence when characters don't match: either skip current character from s (dp[i-1][j]) or skip from t (dp[i][j-1]). We take maximum because we want the longest possible subsequence. These operations are performed repeatedly in the DP algorithm. Time complexity per operation is O(1). Space is negligible. These feed into the recurrence relation: match check decides which case to use, max() computes value for non-match case. Common mistakes: using wrong comparison operator, case sensitivity issues, or incorrect understanding of max() behavior with negative numbers (not applicable here since lengths are non-negative). The pseudocode shows simple if-else with max().

examples.trace:
Character comparison: 'a' == 'A'? In most languages, this is false (case sensitive)
Max operation: max(3,5) = 5 because 5 > 3

In LCS context:
If comparing s[i]='a' and t[j]='A': no match → use max()
If values are dp[i-1][j]=3, dp[i][j-1]=5 → result is 5

Character comparison: 'x' == 'x' → true
Max operation: max(0,0) = 0 (equal values)

In LCS context:
If match → use diagonal value + 1
If no match with both values 0 → result is 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings and Array Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of strings as character sequences and array indexing
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers the prerequisite concepts needed for LCS: strings as sequences of characters, and array indexing to access individual characters. A string is an ordered collection of characters (like 'abc' has characters 'a', 'b', 'c' in positions 0,1,2). We access characters using indices: s[0] gives first character, s[1] second, etc. String length is number of characters (len(s)). Empty string has length 0. In programming, strings are typically immutable. For LCS, we need to compare characters at specific positions from two strings, so understanding indexing is crucial. We also need to understand zero-based indexing (first element at index 0) versus one-based (first at 1). Most programming languages use zero-based. This feeds into basic operations where we compare s[i] and t[j]. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (accessing s[len(s)] which is invalid), confusing length with last index (last index is len(s)-1), or treating strings as mutable. The pseudocode assumes zero-based indexing and shows proper bounds.

examples.trace:
String: "hello"
Length: 5
Indices and characters:
Index 0: 'h'
Index 1: 'e'
Index 2: 'l'
Index 3: 'l'
Index 4: 'o'

Valid indices: 0 through 4
Invalid: s[5] would be out of bounds
Last character: s[4] = 'o'
Second character: s[1] = 'e'

String: "" (empty string)
Length: 0
No characters to access
s[0] would be invalid (index out of bounds)

Comparison: empty string vs "a"
"" has no characters, "a" has one character 'a' at index 0
